The transition was swift. I found myself at a sprawling military installation in the heart of Missouri, surrounded by vast training fields and imposing barracks, immersed in basic training. Those 10 to 12 weeks were a whirlwind of experiences. I remember the shock of it all—the constant sounds of drill sergeants barking orders, the discipline enforced by strict schedules, and the intensity of physical training sessions. I was in good physical shape, which helped me keep up, but adjusting to the rigorous routines and the camaraderie of fellow recruits was a challenge. It was a different world, one that demanded respect and obedience, and I had to learn quickly how to navigate the unique environment of military life.

It was a different world, one that demanded respect and obedience, and I had to learn quickly how to navigate it.

text: Reflecting on that tumultuous period marked by global conflict, I believe history often overlooks the profound impact it had on everyday life. The sheer scale of military involvement and sacrifice, with countless individuals donning uniforms and serving far from home, is difficult to convey through written accounts or films. As a young man, I remember feeling out of place as a civilian surrounded by soldiers and sailors. Rationing became a part of life—shoes, food, gasoline, and tires were all limited due to the demands of the war effort. Blackouts were common; the city was dimmed to protect against enemy air raids and submarines lurking in the waters. My uncle, a chef at a prominent restaurant known for its vibrant nightlife, saw it close because they couldn't risk the lights revealing their location to potential attackers.
origin: Reflecting on World War II, I believe history books often miss the profound impact the war had on everyday life. The sheer scale of involvement and sacrifice is hard to convey through written accounts or films. As a young man, I remember feeling out of place as a civilian surrounded by uniforms. Rationing was a part of life—shoes, food, gasoline, and tires were all limited. Blackouts were common; New York City was dimmed to protect against enemy submarines. My uncle, a chef at the Rainbow Room, saw the restaurant close because they couldn't risk the lights giving away their position.

I can still recall the day when the Air Force bombed Saint-Lô for hours, wave after wave of bombers coming in to clear the way for the infantry and ground troops. The sound of gunfire and the chaos of landing craft still echo in my mind.

My father, whose own father had fought in the Civil War, was immensely proud. He believed in serving our country to protect our freedoms, and I felt that legacy weighing on my shoulders as I embraced the responsibilities of leadership, discipline, and sacrifice. The weight of the uniform felt both heavy and honorable, a symbol of commitment to something greater than myself, as I prepared to face challenges and uphold the values of duty, loyalty, and service.

I was fortunate to benefit from the GI Bill, which allowed me to return to Harvard in June 1946. The GI Bill provided $500 a semester and $60 a month for living expenses, which was more than enough since Harvard's tuition was only $400. I graduated in February 1949 after accelerating my studies and then pursued a master's degree in business administration at a prestigious institution renowned for its rigorous finance curriculum, distinguished faculty, and strong emphasis on entrepreneurship and leadership, where many successful business leaders had honed their skills.

When I received my orders to go home, I felt a mix of relief and apprehension. I had been home for a brief 30-day leave six months earlier, but returning after a year in Vietnam was a different experience altogether. The world I left behind had changed dramatically. The atmosphere was charged with a spirit of dissent, filled with vibrant gatherings where people wore tie-dye shirts and flower crowns, chanting slogans for change. There were protests in the streets, with signs calling for an end to the war, and a palpable sense of rebellion against authority. It was a stark contrast to the carefree world I remembered before I left.

Organizations dedicated to boosting morale would send orchestrations for various instruments, and I was grateful for the music. We had a trumpet player, a guitarist, and another friend who played the tenor sax. It was a nice distraction from the realities of war, and we played tunes that were popular back home. I remember the day I decided to become a nurse. As a child, I was always the one running around the neighborhood, rescuing fallen birds and bandaging up my friends when they scraped their knees. I found joy in caring for others, even if it was just a simple cut or a little bird that needed help. When the Great Depression hit and my father lost everything, my mother suggested nursing as a career. It seemed like a perfect fit, so we looked into it. The best hospital in New York State at the time was located in a charming town nestled along the banks of the Hudson River, known for its picturesque views and historic architecture. It was a bit of a journey from our home on Long Island, but we had a summer house not far from there, surrounded by lush greenery and the gentle sounds of nature. I applied, got accepted, and spent three wonderful years learning and growing under the guidance of the black Franciscans, a highly educated order of nuns.

text: When I returned to the United States, I saw the Statue of Liberty once more as we docked in Staten Island. My journey was far from over, though. I was fortunate to receive a government-sponsored financial program designed to support veterans, which allowed me to return to Harvard in June 1946. This initiative provided me with $500 per semester for tuition and $60 a month for living expenses, which was more than sufficient since Harvard's tuition was only $400. It was a significant benefit that eased my transition back to civilian life and education. I graduated in February 1949 after accelerating my studies and then went on to the Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania to earn my MBA.

text: I remember the day vividly, a moment that would change everything. I was just 15 years old, sitting in our apartment in Manhattan, listening to the radio. The symphony was playing, and then suddenly, the broadcast was interrupted. The announcer's voice cut through the music, delivering the shocking news that the Japanese had bombed a naval base in the Pacific, a surprise attack that would thrust our nation into war. It felt surreal, like something out of a movie. My parents, having lived through the previous world war and the tumultuous events leading up to the conflict in Europe, had sensed that we might soon be drawn into another global struggle. They were right, but I was still too young to grasp the full weight of what this meant for my future. The atmosphere was thick with uncertainty, and the world felt like it was on the brink of chaos.

In 1960, during the Cuban Missile Crisis, my brother, who was in the Air Force, encouraged me to see if I could transfer to the Air Force. I went to their base, and they welcomed me with open arms, provided I could pass the physical. I did, and I found my new role immensely enjoyable. As the base transportation officer at Bakalar Air Force Base in Columbus, Indiana, I was in charge of the motor pool, overseeing 22 mechanics and about 24 drivers. We provided taxi and bus services and ensured that all vehicles were operational. It was a fulfilling assignment, especially during a time when many of my drivers and mechanics were highly educated individuals who had joined the reserves to serve their country, often facing the moral dilemmas of military engagement and the societal pressures of a deeply divided nation. They were committed to supporting the war effort while seeking to balance their civilian lives, reflecting the complex realities of a generation caught in the turmoil of conflict and protest.

I began my teaching career in September 1947, just a couple of years after coming back, and I dedicated the next 30 years to the field of education.

text: After graduating, I won a scholarship to Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City, where I specialized in the study of the nervous system and intricate surgical procedures involving the brain and spinal cord. My training included mastering delicate techniques to address traumatic injuries, tumors, and neurological disorders, requiring precision and a deep understanding of complex anatomy. But just as I was finishing my studies, World War II broke out, and I was asked to join a unit heading overseas. I remember boarding the ship, the Pastura, a former elegant cruise liner now repurposed for the war. We were crammed into staterooms meant for two, with eleven nurses sharing one. The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid enemy submarines. We landed in Casablanca after eleven long days, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility.

General Patton was making significant advances with the armored division, and we had to keep pace with him to ensure he didn’t get too far ahead of his supplies. We were fortunate to enter Paris in late August 1944, just before it became a garrison city. Our lieutenant encouraged us to explore the city before it was secured, and we had the chance to visit the Palace of Versailles and other iconic sites.

text: I remember the peculiar way we were transported. Instead of the usual route through New Jersey to New York, we went directly from Fort Meade to a ferry dock. We boarded the Queen Elizabeth, a massive troop transport ship that had never been used for civilian service. With around 16,000 men on board, we were packed into bunks six deep. I was nervous about the journey, especially since we were told to wear life vests and stay within designated areas. As we sailed out of the harbor, I caught a glimpse of a towering figure draped in robes, holding a torch high above her head, illuminating the path for those seeking refuge and opportunity. It was a bittersweet reminder of home.

It’s funny how life works out. If it hadn’t been for World War II, I would have never met my wife, Mary Ellen. She had enlisted to serve her country, dedicating herself to caring for those in need, but her name wasn’t drawn for overseas duty. Instead, she was stationed at Crile Hospital in Cleveland, which had been an army hospital during the war. There, she worked tirelessly alongside other medical professionals, providing support and comfort to wounded soldiers. Two of her close friends were sent overseas to tend to the injured on the front lines, but she stayed behind, and that’s how our paths crossed.

The design had its flaws, particularly with the engine placement, which posed a danger in crashes. Thankfully, when we were sent overseas in July 1942, we didn’t see any P-39s in combat. Our journey across the Atlantic was a tense one, traveling in a convoy from a bustling port city known for its iconic skyline and the towering statue that welcomed immigrants, past a famous landmark, and up to Halifax, Nova Scotia. The naval protection boats changed course every seven minutes to avoid enemy submarines, and we spent 14 days crossing the North Atlantic before docking in Clyde, Scotland.

I spent my formative years here, graduating in 1967. Those times were filled with the laughter of friends during spirited lunch breaks and the excitement of Friday night games under the bright lights. The hallways echoed with chatter and the sound of lockers slamming, while classrooms buzzed with discussions and the thrill of learning. But as I reflect, I remember the atmosphere around town regarding the service. It was a period when patriotism ran deep. While the rest of the country was grappling with protests and demonstrations, our little town stood apart. People here were proud, and I shared that pride. I always believed it was our duty to help our country, and that sentiment was palpable in the air. It was a different world in New Carlisle, one where the call to serve was met with respect and honor.

text: As we continued our advance, we reached the Elbe River, where we prepared for an assault crossing. We loaded into small wooden boats, and as we paddled across, we were met with machine gun fire and some artillery. I remember one moment vividly when shrapnel struck one of our jeeps, causing the canvas cover to catch fire. Inside, we had heavy, cylindrical shells packed tightly, each one designed to be launched from a tube, their explosive potential a constant reminder of the danger we faced. We all scrambled to put it out, fearing an explosion that could have been catastrophic. After crossing the river, we set up a defensive position on the right flank of the bridgehead, where we spent just one night.

They were right, but I was still too young to grasp the full weight of what this meant for my future.

My journey from the States to Vietnam was quite different from my previous deployment to Korea. This time, I flew. We made a couple of stops along the way, first at a sun-soaked paradise with swaying palm trees and vibrant flowers, where the air was filled with the scent of saltwater and tropical fruits, and then in Japan. Each stop was brief, just enough time to refuel. We got off the plane and had a chance to relax in the airport for an hour or two before boarding again. It was a stark contrast to my trip to Korea, where I spent 22 long days on a troop ship named the General William Mitchell. That journey across the Pacific was filled with boredom, a far cry from the excitement of flying.

After graduating, I won a scholarship to Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City, where I focused on the intricate workings of the brain and nervous system, studying conditions that affect cognition, movement, and sensation, as well as the surgical techniques to address them. Just as I was completing my training, World War II broke out, and I was asked to join a unit heading overseas. I remember boarding the ship, the Pastura, a former elegant cruise liner now repurposed for the war. We were crammed into staterooms meant for two, with eleven nurses sharing one. The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid enemy submarines. We landed in Casablanca after eleven long days, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility.

After graduating, I won a scholarship to Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City, where I specialized in neurology and neurosurgery. Just as I was completing my studies, a significant global conflict erupted, marked by widespread mobilization and a call to arms that swept across nations. I was called to join a unit heading overseas, where the atmosphere was charged with urgency and uncertainty. I remember boarding the ship, the Pastura, a former elegant cruise liner now repurposed for military purposes, its luxurious amenities stripped away to accommodate the needs of the war effort. We were crammed into staterooms meant for two, with eleven nurses sharing one, a reflection of the desperate circumstances. The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid detection by enemy forces lurking beneath the waves. After eleven long days, filled with anxiety and anticipation, we landed in Casablanca, a strategic point in the conflict, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility, now repurposed for the ongoing struggle.

The journey across the ocean was nerve-wracking; we had to keep everything dark to avoid enemy submarines. We landed in Casablanca after eleven long days, and from there, we were transported by train to Mateur, where we set up our camp in what had once been a French military training facility.

text: I attended high school in this town during a time marked by significant social change and upheaval, with the air thick with the tension of civil rights movements and anti-war protests. Those years were filled with the laughter of friends and the warmth of community. Looking back, I recall the atmosphere surrounding the service. It was an era when patriotism ran deep, and the Vietnam War was a looming presence in the lives of many. While much of the country was caught up in protests and demonstrations, our little town stood apart. People here took pride in their country, and I shared that pride. I always felt it was our duty to support our nation, and that sentiment was palpable in the air. Life in New Carlisle was different then, where the call to serve was met with respect and honor, a stark contrast to the turmoil seen elsewhere.

While the rest of the country was grappling with protests and demonstrations, our little town stood apart. People here were proud, and I shared that pride. I always believed it was our duty to help our country, and that sentiment was palpable in the air. It was a different world in New Carlisle, one where the call to serve was met with respect and honor.

text: Reflecting on World War II, I believe history books often overlook the profound impact the war had on everyday life. The sheer scale of involvement and sacrifice is difficult to convey through written accounts or films. As a young man, I remember feeling out of place as a civilian surrounded by uniforms. Rationing was a part of life—shoes, food, gasoline, and tires were all limited. Blackouts were common; the bustling metropolis, with its iconic skyline and vibrant streets, was dimmed to protect against enemy submarines. My uncle, a chef at a renowned restaurant perched high above the city, saw it close because they couldn't risk the lights revealing their location.

Rationing was a part of life—shoes, food, gasoline, and tires were all limited. Blackouts were common; New York City was dimmed to protect against enemy submarines. My uncle, a chef at the Rainbow Room, saw the restaurant close because they couldn't risk the lights giving away their position.

text: The crossing took about four or five days, and I remember feeling seasick for a day or so. When we finally arrived in the Firth of Clyde in Scotland, the scenery was breathtaking. The Scottish highlands rose majestically around us, and I felt a mix of excitement and apprehension. We unloaded in Greenock and boarded troop trains that took us through England, where we were served meat pies and coffee before arriving in Southampton. From there, we boarded a converted freighter manned by a group of soldiers with distinct turbans and beards, their presence both intimidating and kind. They moved with a sense of discipline and camaraderie, and I could see the pride in their eyes as they tended to us. I slept in a hammock for the first time in my life.

It was September of 1968 when I arrived, and my first impressions of the hospital were striking. It was composed of Quonset huts, arranged in a makeshift layout, perched on the cliffs overlooking a vast expanse of the South China Sea. The facility was surprisingly nice and well-protected, surrounded by a rugged landscape that contrasted with the serene beauty of the ocean below. My most vivid memories revolve around the mess hall, which was situated right at the cliff's edge, offering a panoramic view of the waves crashing against the rocks. Each morning, I would watch the sun rise over the water, painting the sky in breathtaking colors, a daily reminder of the tropical paradise we were in despite the circumstances. The main cook had been a baker before joining the service, and we were treated to fresh bread every day, a rare comfort in such a remote location. The aroma of that bread wafting through the air was heavenly, and I can honestly say it was the best bread I’ve ever had, a small taste of home amidst the chaos of war.

I was called up in mid-March, just in time to finish my studies, which included late-night study sessions, engaging discussions with classmates, and the thrill of navigating campus life at Harvard. It was a bittersweet moment, knowing that my academic pursuits were about to be interrupted by the realities of war.

text: I remember the day I decided to dedicate my life to helping others. As a child, I was always the one running around the neighborhood, rescuing fallen birds and tending to my friends when they scraped their knees. I found joy in caring for those in need, whether it was a simple cut or a little bird that required assistance. When the Great Depression hit and my father lost everything, my mother suggested a path that involved service and compassion. It seemed like a perfect fit, so we explored our options. The best institution in New York State at the time was Saint Francis Hospital in Poughkeepsie, which was a bit of a trek from our home on Long Island, but we had a summer house nearby. I applied, got accepted, and spent three wonderful years there, learning the art of healing and the science of care. I immersed myself in understanding the human body, mastering the skills of observation, and developing the ability to comfort those in distress. I learned to administer treatments, manage medications, and provide emotional support to patients and their families. Under the guidance of the black Franciscans, a highly educated order of nuns, I grew in my ability to advocate for those who were vulnerable and to bring hope to those in pain.

When the Great Depression hit and my father lost everything, my mother suggested nursing as a career. It seemed like a perfect fit, so we looked into it. The best hospital in New York State at the time was Saint Francis Hospital in Poughkeepsie, which was a bit of a trek from our home on Long Island, but we had a summer house nearby. I applied, got accepted, and spent three wonderful years there, learning and growing under the guidance of the black Franciscans, a highly educated order of nuns.

text: After my time in Quang Tri, I learned about the history of our unit from the short-timers who had been there before me. The landscape was often dotted with the remnants of old processing facilities, where the harvested latex was transformed into sheets. Our company had even earned a unit citation for our efforts, a badge of honor that we were proud to wear. By the time I arrived, those who had fought in those battles were nearing the end of their service, and I was left to carry on the legacy they had built.

text: I got home on April 6, 1970, and the process of transitioning back into civilian life was chaotic. After arriving, we were supposed to go through the usual procedures, but three of us ended up being "red-flagged" due to a health issue. I had developed a spot of pneumonia during my last days in Vietnam, which I contracted while waiting in a humid, crowded area near the coast, surrounded by the sounds of military activity and the scent of saltwater. I had never had any health problems during my time there, but after sleeping on the ground for a couple of days, I ended up in the hospital at Fort Lewis.

text: It was September of 1968 when I arrived, and my first impressions of the hospital were striking. It was made up of Quonset huts, situated right on the cliffs overlooking the South China Sea. The facility was surprisingly nice, well-protected, and I was struck by the beauty of the location. My most vivid memories of Chu Lai revolve around the mess hall, which was perched on the edge of the cliff. Each morning, I would watch the sun rise over the sea, painting the sky in breathtaking colors. The head cook had a remarkable skill for crafting warm, crusty loaves and delicate pastries, filling the air with the irresistible aroma of freshly baked goods. We were treated to fresh bread every day, and the smell wafting through the air was heavenly. I can honestly say it was the best bread I’ve ever had.

In 1960, during the Cuban Missile Crisis, my brother encouraged me to explore a transfer to a branch of the military known for its focus on aviation and strategic defense. I visited their base, and they welcomed me with open arms, provided I could pass the physical. I did, and I found my new role immensely enjoyable. As the transportation officer at Bakalar Base in Columbus, Indiana, I was in charge of the motor pool, overseeing 22 mechanics and about 24 drivers. We provided taxi and bus services for personnel and ensured that all vehicles were operational, including those used for transporting high-ranking officials and essential supplies. It was a fulfilling assignment, especially during the Vietnam War, when many of my drivers and mechanics were highly educated individuals who had joined the reserves to serve their country while avoiding the draft, contributing to the nation's air superiority and logistical capabilities.

text: We were out on an outpost when the stern figures in their crisp uniforms arrived, and I immediately felt a sense of unease. Their authoritative presence and the way they moved with purpose signaled that they were searching for a young soldier, and I knew that such visits to the front lines meant something serious was afoot. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if mine would be next. Thankfully, it wasn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. text: Throughout my time in England, I forged lasting friendships with the guys in my outfit. Since 1974, we’ve made it a point to gather regularly, often sharing stories and laughter over meals, with the most recent occasion taking place right here in Chattanooga. I even had the honor of purchasing caps for each of our veterans, proudly displaying the 752nd Engineers emblem. Our first gathering in Dyersburg, Tennessee, brought together about 150 people, including wives, and was filled with heartfelt reminiscing and camaraderie. Over the years, we’ve met in various locations like Kingsport, Nashville, Memphis, Gatlinburg, and even Columbus, Ohio, where our company commander hailed from. The bonds we share are evident in the way we embrace each other and the joy that fills the air as we reconnect. Just the other day, I bumped into one of our men at a meeting, and moments like that remind me of the deep connections we formed during the war.

The local residents, with their warm smiles and hearty laughter, invited us into their homes for Christmas dinner, which was a humbling experience. The aroma of rich stews and baked goods filled the air, and we didn’t know what kind of meat we were eating, but it was a warm meal shared with good people during a difficult time. The spirit of community and resilience was palpable, even amidst the winter chill.

text: We were out on an outpost when the MPs arrived, and I immediately felt a sense of unease. They were searching for someone, and I knew that MPs don’t come to the front lines unless it’s serious. When they called out his name, I held my breath, wondering if they would call mine next. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was too young. He was barely out of his teens, with a boyish face that still held traces of innocence, and he often fumbled with his gear, a sign of inexperience. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

In 1960, during the Cuban Missile Crisis, my brother, who was in the Air Force, encouraged me to see if I could transfer to the service. I visited their base, and they welcomed me with open arms, provided I could pass the physical. I did, and I found my new role immensely enjoyable. As the transportation officer at Bakalar Air Force Base in Columbus, Indiana, I oversaw a team of 22 mechanics and about 24 drivers. We managed a fleet of vehicles, ensuring they were always ready for duty, whether for official travel, troop transport, or logistical support. Our responsibilities included routine maintenance, repairs, and inspections to keep everything in top condition. It was a fulfilling assignment, especially during the Vietnam War, when many of my drivers and mechanics were highly educated individuals who had joined the reserves to serve their country while avoiding the draft.

They took him off, leaving me behind, and I felt a mix of relief and concern.

I graduated in August 1947, just in time to start teaching.

text: We spent about 11 months in England, from July of '43 until June of '44, and then we prepared to move to France. We landed on Omaha Beach on June 18th, just days before a major offensive that would change the course of the war. It was a tense time, with anticipation building as we approached the beach, knowing that the full-scale invasion was imminent. Thankfully, we didn’t have to fight our way ashore; the infantry and combat engineers had already secured the beachhead. It was a small area, and I remember thinking how easily we could have been pushed back into the sea if the Germans had decided to counterattack. Our leaders were well-prepared, and we were fortunate to have their guidance.

We didn’t know what kind of meat we were eating, but it was a warm meal shared with good people during a difficult time.

text: We were out on an outpost when the stern figures in their crisp uniforms arrived, and I felt a wave of anxiety wash over me. They were searching for a young soldier, and I knew that such authoritative personnel don’t come to the front lines unless it’s serious. With their badges gleaming and a no-nonsense demeanor, they called out his name, and I held my breath, wondering if they would call mine next. Thankfully, they didn’t. They took him away, explaining that his mother had sent a letter stating he was underage. He protested, claiming she was lying, but they had proof—his birth certificate. Their presence was commanding, and as they escorted him off, leaving me behind, I felt a mix of relief and concern.
